title: What CES Medical is about
caption:
Cataracts. Eyelid surgery. Glaucoma. Dry eye.

Behind every condition is a question someone has been sitting on for too long.

We are here to answer them. In Kent, close to home, with the same consultants and the same standards whether you come to us through the NHS or privately.

Follow us for honest conversations about eye health in Kent.

title: Do cataracts only affect older people?
caption:
Cataracts are most common after 60, but they can develop at any age, triggered by injury, certain medications or underlying health conditions.

If your vision feels cloudy or like you are looking through a smudged lens, it is worth getting checked. Whatever your age.
notes:
Myth vs fact carousel. Stock photography. Brief the design team. 5 to 6 slides. Clinical review required — confirm all claims about cataract risk factors are accurate. No video asset needed.

Note for designer: Slide 4 wording has been updated to avoid repeating the smudged lens metaphor from the caption — use "Blurry, hazy or washed-out vision?" instead. Slide 5 should display the URL as plain text without the https:// prefix.

title: What is a cataract?
caption:
That foggy, hazy feeling when your vision is not quite right? It could be a cataract.

In 60 seconds, we break down exactly what is happening inside your eye, what it feels like and what treating it involves.

Visit cespatientinformation.co.uk to find out more about cataracts and what treatment involves.

title: Meet Mr Nick Kopsachilis
caption:
Mr Nick Kopsachilis is a Consultant Ophthalmic Surgeon specialising in corneal and cataract surgery. He holds an MD, PhD and a Postgraduate Diploma in Cataract and Refractive Surgery, awarded with distinction. He trained in Munich, at Moorfields Eye Hospital and at King's College London before joining CES Medical.

He has authored more than 80 peer-reviewed publications and leads the corneal service at East Kent Hospitals NHS Foundation Trust.

He sees patients across both NHS and private pathways at Headcorn, Pantiles, Chatham and Southborough. The same standards, regardless of how you come to us.

Questions about cataract or corneal surgery? We are always happy to talk.

title: What is a monofocal lens?
caption:
A monofocal lens corrects vision at one distance. Most people choose distance vision and use reading glasses for close-up work.

It is reliable, well-established and the lens included in standard cataract surgery. Not every patient needs a premium lens. What matters is that the choice fits your life.

Swipe through to see who monofocal lenses suit.

title: NHS vs private cataract surgery: what actually changes?
caption:
NHS cataract waiting times have stretched in many areas of Kent. Going private with CES means you can be assessed promptly and have surgery planned around your schedule.

The consultants are the same. The clinical standards are the same. What changes is the timeline, the time at consultation and the range of lens options available to you.

If you have been on an NHS list and want to understand your options, a conversation costs nothing.
notes:
Clean single image. Teal background. Two-column comparison: NHS pathway vs CES private pathway. Timeline, lens choice, consultation length. No language suggesting private is superior in quality. The comparison must be readable at mobile scale — minimum font size for body text in the comparison columns. Test at 375px width before finalising.

Content for the two-column comparison:

NHS pathway:
- Waiting time: varies by trust, often 6 months or more in Kent
- Lens options: standard monofocal lens
- Consultation: allocated time within a busy clinic
- Schedule: determined by NHS list position

CES Medical private pathway:
- Waiting time: assessed promptly, surgery planned around your schedule
- Lens options: monofocal, multifocal, EDOF and toric lenses available
- Consultation: dedicated time with your consultant
- Schedule: agreed with you directly

Note: design must not make the private column appear superior in clinical quality. Both columns should be visually equal in weight. The differences shown are about access and choice, not about the quality of care.

Design brief using NK-B3 answer as clinical source. Confirm NHS waiting time data is current at time of posting — check with CES admin. Clinical review required.

title: Could you be glasses-free after cataract surgery?
caption:
Imagine reading a menu, driving and watching television without reaching for your glasses. For many cataract patients, a multifocal lens makes that possible.

Multifocal lenses give vision at multiple distances, and many patients find they no longer need glasses after surgery.

They are not right for everyone. Some people notice haloes or reduced contrast in low light. We will always tell you honestly whether a multifocal lens suits your eyes and your lifestyle before recommending one.

Swipe through for what to expect.

Questions? Call 01732 757771 or visit cespatientinformation.co.uk/cataract-surgery

title: Which cataract lens is right for you?
caption:
Monofocal, multifocal, EDOF, toric. The choice can feel overwhelming.

The honest answer is: the right lens depends on your eyes, your lifestyle, your hobbies and your expectations. Not on what looks best on a comparison chart.

Swipe through our breakdown. Then bring your questions to the consultation.

Questions before you book? Call 01732 757771 or visit cespatientinformation.co.uk/cataract-surgery
notes:
Carousel. 6 slides. IG FB LI. Source: NK-C9 and NK-C10 (Mr Kopsachilis on lens choice), KQ-C8 (Mr Qureshi on lens selection).

PLATFORM NOTE: If a consultant video clip is used as a slide, the Instagram and Facebook versions can include it. The LinkedIn version must be a PDF document carousel and cannot contain video. Replace any video slide in the LinkedIn version with a static still and quote.

Slide 1: Which cataract lens is right for you?

Slide 2: Monofocal. Corrects one distance, usually distance vision. Reading glasses still needed for close-up tasks. Some patients choose near focus depending on their occupation. Well-established. Reliable.

Slide 3: Multifocal. Corrects multiple distances. Many patients find they need glasses far less or not at all. Not for everyone.

Slide 4: EDOF (Extended Depth of Focus). Extended intermediate range. Fewer haloes than full multifocal. Some may still need reading glasses.

Slide 5: Toric. Corrects distance vision and astigmatism at the same time. Astigmatism is a common condition where the cornea is slightly irregular, causing blurry or distorted vision. A toric design can also be combined with multifocal technology for patients who want to correct both astigmatism and reading distance. Your consultant will advise at assessment.

Slide 6: The right lens is the one that fits your life. That conversation starts at consultation. Find out more at cespatientinformation.co.uk/cataract-surgery or call 01732 757771.

title: Is your eyelid problem medical or cosmetic?
caption:
Drooping upper eyelids that cut into your field of vision are a medical issue, not a cosmetic one. That distinction matters for how the procedure is approached and, in many cases, whether your insurance covers it.

Here is how we tell the difference at consultation.

If your eyelid is affecting your vision, call 01732 757771 to find out whether it is functional or cosmetic. In many cases functional surgery is covered by private medical insurance.
notes:
Carousel. 6 slides. IG FB LI. Source: NK-C5 (Mr Kopsachilis on oculoplastic vs cosmetic surgeon) and NK-C6 (what is ptosis).

PLATFORM NOTE: If a video clip of Mr Kopsachilis is used as a slide, the Instagram and Facebook versions can include it. The LinkedIn version must be a PDF document carousel and cannot contain video. Replace any video slide in the LinkedIn version with a static still and a consultant quote.

This post unlocks insurance funding awareness. The medical vs cosmetic distinction is commercially valuable because it tells patients who assumed their only option was self-funded cosmetic surgery that functional eyelid surgery may be covered by insurance.

Slide 1: Is your eyelid problem medical or cosmetic?

Slide 2: Ptosis is the medical term for a drooping upper eyelid. When it drops far enough to affect your vision, it is a medical condition.

Slide 3: Functional eyelid surgery repairs a condition affecting your sight. It may be covered by private medical insurance.

Slide 4: Cosmetic eyelid surgery (blepharoplasty) improves appearance. It is self-funded.

Slide 5: The same surgeon. The same skill. But a different pathway, a different consent process and sometimes a different funder.

Slide 6: At consultation we assess which applies to you. No assumption, no pressure. Find out more at cespatientinformation.co.uk or call 01732 757771.

title: Thinking about laser eye surgery? After 45, lens surgery may suit you better
caption:
If you are over 45 and tired of depending on glasses, you may have looked into laser eye surgery. But laser is not the only route to reducing your dependence on glasses, and for many people over 45 it is not the best one.

Lens-based surgery, the same technology used in cataract surgery, can correct your vision and, for most people, prevent cataracts from ever developing later. Here is how the options compare.

Swipe through. If you are weighing up your options, call 01732 757771 to talk it through.
notes:
SLIDES (7):

Slide 1: Thinking about laser eye surgery? After 45, lens surgery may suit you better.

Slide 2: Laser eye surgery reshapes the cornea to correct vision. It works well for many younger people with stable prescriptions.

Slide 3: But laser does not address the lens of the eye, which continues to change with age. Many people who have laser in their thirties still develop cataracts and need lens surgery later.

Slide 4: Refractive lens exchange replaces the eye's natural lens with a clear artificial lens. It corrects vision and, because the natural lens is removed, you will never develop a cataract in that eye.

Slide 5: This is the same proven technology used in cataract surgery, with the same lens options: monofocal, multifocal, extended depth of focus and toric for astigmatism.

Slide 6: Which is right for you depends on your age, your prescription, your eye health and how you use your eyes. That is what a consultation is for. We will tell you honestly which option suits you, including if that is neither.

Slide 7: CES Medical offers lens-based vision correction with consultant-led care across Kent. Find out more at cespatientinformation.co.uk or call 01732 757771.

PRODUCTION NOTES:

Carousel. 7 slides. IG FB X. Production: stock or custom graphic. Lead: external.

IMPORTANT: This post must not imply CES offers laser refractive surgery (LASIK, LASEK, PRK). CES does not. The post acknowledges laser eye surgery exists, then honestly positions CES's actual service, lens-based vision correction (refractive lens exchange and premium lenses), as an alternative that may suit the over-45 audience better. The framing is comparison and honesty, not a claim that CES does laser.

CLINICAL CONFIRMATION REQUIRED: Confirm with the clinical team that CES offers refractive lens exchange (RLE) as a private service before publishing. If CES offers RLE, this post is accurate. If CES only offers lens replacement in the context of cataract surgery (not elective RLE for vision correction), Slide 4 must be adjusted to reflect that and the post repositioned around cataract lens options only.

Tone: honest, educational, not salesy. The line on Slide 6 (we will tell you honestly which option suits you, including if that is neither) is the CES brand voice and must stay. It protects credibility by acknowledging surgery is not always the answer.

No outcome guarantees. The statement that removing the natural lens prevents a cataract forming in that eye is a statement of fact about the procedure, not an outcome promise about vision.

Caption as above. Lens education connects this post to P05, P08, P09, P15 and P58.

It is worth raising at your consultation. Your consultant will assess whether a toric lens is right for your eyes.

Find out more at cespatientinformation.co.uk/cataract-surgery or call 01732 757771.

title: What does FRCOphth mean for you as a patient?
caption:
Many of our consultant surgeons carry FRCOphth after their names. It stands for Fellow of the Royal College of Ophthalmologists, the highest professional qualification for eye surgeons in the UK.

It is not honorary. It requires years of supervised surgical training, written and practical examinations and ongoing revalidation. Here is what that means in practical terms for your care.

Some of our consultants trained internationally and hold equivalent senior qualifications such as an MD and PhD. What matters is that every CES consultant surgeon has completed the rigorous training their specialty demands.
notes:
Single image, or two to three slides if a carousel version is preferred for LinkedIn. LI and IG. Source: KQ-B2 (Mr Qureshi on revalidation and standards), NK-B1 (Mr Kopsachilis on why credentials matter to patients).

Credential explained simply, no jargon. End with: When you choose a CES consultant, you know exactly what level of training sits behind that appointment.

ACCURACY NOTE: Not every CES consultant surgeon holds FRCOphth. Mr Qureshi and Mr Shahid hold FRCOphth. Mr Kopsachilis holds an MD, PhD and other senior qualifications from an internationally trained pathway. The caption must say many of our consultant surgeons, not all, and must acknowledge the internationally trained pathway. Do not imply every consultant holds the same UK qualification.

Visual concept: Clean, professional. The FRCOphth credential explained in plain English. CES Medical logo. If a carousel version: Slide 1 the question, Slide 2 what FRCOphth involves, Slide 3 the closing line and CTA.

title: Glaucoma rarely gives any warning
caption:
Glaucoma rarely has symptoms in its early stages. It damages the optic nerve gradually and, without regular monitoring, the first sign is often vision that cannot be restored.

That is what makes regular eye examinations so important, particularly after 40. We diagnose and manage glaucoma at all our centres, including laser treatment where appropriate.
notes: Glaucoma awareness carousel. Design team brief. Stock medical imagery or glaucoma diagram. Clinical review required — confirm glaucoma screening guidance aligns with current NICE guidelines. No video.

Slide 1: Why does the second eye matter as much as the first?

Slide 3: After the first eye is treated, the visual imbalance between the two eyes can affect depth perception, balance and driving.

title: Functional or cosmetic? The eyelid question that changes everything
caption:
The difference between a functional and a cosmetic eyelid procedure is not always obvious. Even to the patient.

We see this regularly at our Tunbridge Wells and Headcorn clinics. A patient books thinking they want cosmetic blepharoplasty. At consultation, we discover the drooping lid is cutting into their visual field. That changes everything: the pathway, the consent process and in many cases the funding.

Swipe through for how we distinguish between the two and what it means for referral and treatment.

How often do you see patients who have self-diagnosed a cosmetic issue that turns out to have a functional component? It would be useful to understand how common this is from a GP perspective.
linkedInHook: The difference between a functional and a cosmetic eyelid procedure is not always obvious. Even to the patient.
notes:
Slides: (1) Functional or cosmetic? The eyelid question that changes everything. (2) Ptosis is the medical term for a drooping upper eyelid. When the lid drops far enough to obstruct the superior visual field, it becomes a medical condition with clinical consequences. (3) Functional ptosis repair: medical indication, affects vision or visual field, may be covered by PMI, different consent and pathway to cosmetic procedures. (4) Cosmetic blepharoplasty: elective, aesthetic outcome, self-funded, patient driven by appearance rather than visual function. (5) Same surgeon. Same operating standards. Entirely different clinical and funding pathway. (6) At consultation we assess: the degree of ptosis, the marginal reflex distance, the impact on the superior visual field and the patient's primary concern (vision or appearance). title: Glaucoma has no symptoms in the early stages
caption:
Glaucoma has no symptoms in the early stages. That is what makes it dangerous.

By the time a patient notices a change in their peripheral vision, some damage has already occurred. And unlike a cataract, that damage cannot be reversed. It can only be slowed.

Swipe through for what we look for at a glaucoma assessment at CES Medical and who should be prioritised for screening.

In your experience, what is the most common barrier to earlier glaucoma detection in your patient population? We are trying to understand where the referral pathway breaks down.
linkedInHook: Glaucoma has no symptoms in the early stages. That is what makes it dangerous.
notes:
PDF document carousel. 10 slides. LinkedIn brand account. Teal cover. Audience: GPs, optometrists and clinical referrers.

Slide 1: Glaucoma has no symptoms in the early stages. That is what makes it dangerous.

Slide 2: What glaucoma does. In the most common form, open-angle glaucoma, it damages the optic nerve through raised intraocular pressure. The peripheral vision goes first, slowly, without pain. Central vision is typically preserved until the condition is advanced.

Slide 3: Who is at higher risk. Anyone over 40. Anyone with a first-degree relative with glaucoma (three to four times higher risk). Patients with African or Caribbean heritage (six times more likely to develop the condition). Patients with high myopia (typically over -6 dioptres). Those with elevated IOP detected at a routine optician visit.

Slide 4: What a CES glaucoma assessment includes. IOP measurement. Optic nerve imaging. Visual field testing. Pachymetry (corneal thickness, which affects IOP interpretation). Clinical examination.

Slide 5: Management options we offer. Topical drops. Selective laser trabeculoplasty (SLT), a five-minute outpatient laser procedure that lowers IOP in many patients without the compliance burden of daily drops. Surgical options for advanced cases.

Slide 6: SLT at CES. SLT is clinically effective, quick and well-tolerated. Many patients who struggle with drop compliance respond very well. We offer it at Chatham and Headcorn.

Slide 7: The referral threshold. If an optician report flags raised IOP, cup-to-disc ratio changes or visual field defects, a specialist assessment is appropriate. Do not wait for a second optician visit to confirm.

Slide 8: For patients. If you have not had a full eye examination in the past two years and you are over 40, that is the starting point. An optician can identify the early signs before a specialist is needed.

Slide 9: We accept both NHS and self-funded private referrals for glaucoma. Most PMI policies cover glaucoma management including monitoring and laser treatment.

Slide 10: CES Medical. Glaucoma diagnosis and management across Kent. cesmedical.co.uk

title: 2RT nanopulse laser for dry AMD
caption:
There is a laser procedure for dry AMD that very few specialists in the UK currently offer.

Dry age-related macular degeneration affects the central vision. For most patients, the advice has historically been: manage your risk factors and wait. The wet form has treatments. The dry form, until recently, has had very few.

The 2RT nanopulse laser changes that picture for some patients. Mr Kashif Qureshi at CES Medical is one of a very small number of specialists in the UK with experience using it.

Swipe through for what 2RT is, who it suits and what the evidence currently shows.

How often do you have conversations with dry AMD patients where the available options feel inadequate? We would be interested to understand whether this is a gap GPs and optometrists encounter regularly.
linkedInHook: There is a laser procedure for dry AMD that almost no one in the UK knows exists.
notes:
PDF document carousel. 10 slides. LinkedIn brand account. Teal cover. Audience: GPs, optometrists and patients aged 45 to 65 with dry AMD diagnosis.

SCHEDULING FLAG, DO NOT REMOVE: This post must not be scheduled until Mr Kashif Qureshi has confirmed in writing that he is comfortable with CES Medical publishing this content on the brand account. The 2RT content is directly associated with his name and practice. Post remains in Draft status until confirmation is received. Once confirmed, add the confirmation date to these production notes before scheduling.

Slide 1: There is a laser treatment for dry AMD that very few specialists in the UK currently offer.

Slide 2: Age-related macular degeneration (AMD) is the leading cause of sight loss in the UK in people over 50. It affects the macula, the central part of the retina responsible for detailed vision.

Slide 3: Wet AMD has established treatments: intravitreal injections that slow or stop the progression of abnormal blood vessel growth. Most patients are familiar with this pathway.

Slide 4: Dry AMD has historically had far fewer options. Lifestyle factors, vitamins and monitoring. That has been the standard approach for most patients.

Slide 5: 2RT (Retinal Rejuvenation Therapy) is a nanopulse laser treatment that targets the retinal pigment epithelium without causing thermal damage to surrounding tissue. Clinical studies suggest it may slow the progression of early dry AMD in suitable patients.

Slide 6: Who may be suitable. Patients with early to intermediate dry AMD. Patients whose progression is documented over time. Patients who have been told there is nothing further to offer them. Not all dry AMD patients are candidates. A specialist assessment is needed.

Slide 7: What the evidence shows. A randomised controlled trial (RESTORE) demonstrated reduced drusen area and some evidence of slowed progression in treated eyes. The evidence base is developing. We present it honestly, not as a cure.

Slide 8: What this means in practice. For patients with dry AMD who have been told there are no treatment options, a conversation with a specialist who offers 2RT is at minimum worth having. It may not be appropriate for every patient, but the option should be known.

Slide 9: Availability. 2RT is not widely available in the UK. The assessment for suitability is carried out at consultation. If you are a GP or optometrist with a patient who may be appropriate, we are happy to discuss.

title: Vitreous floaters: when to refer and when to reassure
caption:
Floaters are one of the most common eye symptoms GPs see, and most of the time they are benign. But a small number signal a retinal tear or detachment, where hours matter.

This is a quick reference on when to reassure and when to refer urgently, built for primary care.

If you ever have a patient where you are uncertain, we are happy to take a call. An informal conversation is quicker than you might expect and can save a patient's sight.

How do you currently handle the sudden-onset floaters presentation in your practice? We are interested in where the referral pathway could be smoother.
linkedInHook: Most floaters are harmless. Some are the first sign of a retinal tear. Knowing which is which is the difference between reassurance and a sight-saving referral.
notes:
Document carousel. 10 slides. LinkedIn brand account. Educational pillar. Audience: GPs and primary care. PDF document carousel, no video, LinkedIn-compatible.

CLINICAL REVIEW REQUIRED: This post advises GPs on a referral decision involving a potential sight-threatening emergency. Mr Syed Shahid must review the clinical content for accuracy before publishing. The red flag triad and the same-day urgency message must be clinically correct and unambiguous.

This post pairs with P137 (retinal detachment long-form video) and P153 (retinal detachment Short). Floaters are the symptom that connects them. The referral guidance here must be consistent with the urgent-care message in those posts: red flag symptoms go to eye casualty the same day, not to a private booking.

Slide content:

Slide 1: Vitreous floaters: when to refer and when to reassure. A quick reference for primary care.

Slide 2: Most floaters are benign. They are usually caused by age-related changes in the vitreous gel, including posterior vitreous detachment (PVD), which is a normal part of ageing.

Slide 3: The reassuring picture. Floaters that have been stable for a long time. A small number of floaters that drift in and out of vision. No flashes, no shadow, no sudden change. These patients can usually be reassured and reviewed routinely.

Slide 4: The red flags. Refer urgently if the patient reports: a sudden increase in floaters, especially a shower or cluster appearing at once. Flashes of light (photopsia). A dark shadow or curtain across part of the visual field. Any loss of vision.

Slide 5: Why the red flags matter. A sudden increase in floaters with flashes can indicate a retinal tear. If a tear progresses to retinal detachment, the window for sight-preserving treatment is short. This is a same-day concern, not a routine referral.

Slide 6: What to do with a red flag presentation. The patient should be seen the same day. Direct them to the nearest eye casualty department, or contact the on-call ophthalmology service. Do not wait for a routine referral pathway when red flags are present.

Slide 7: When it is not urgent but warrants review. New floaters without red flags still warrant an optometry or ophthalmology assessment to confirm a benign cause and exclude a tear. Routine, but not ignored.

Slide 8: How CES can help. For non-urgent floaters, we assess and reassure with dilated examination. For uncertain presentations, call us. Mr Syed Shahid, our Consultant Ophthalmologist and Vitreoretinal Surgeon, leads our vitreoretinal service.

Slide 9: The principle. Most floaters are benign. The clinical skill is recognising the few that are not. When in doubt, a sudden change with flashes or shadow is urgent until proven otherwise.

Slide 10: CES Medical. Vitreoretinal services across Kent. cesmedical.co.uk

title: The case for SLT as first-line treatment in newly diagnosed glaucoma
caption:
When a patient is newly diagnosed with open-angle glaucoma, the traditional first step has been daily eye drops. The evidence base has shifted, and current NICE guidance now reflects that.

This is a short summary for primary care and optometry colleagues on where first-line treatment stands, based on the LiGHT trial and NICE guideline NG81.

It is not that drops are wrong. It is that for many patients, selective laser trabeculoplasty is now a recommended first option, with advantages in compliance and quality of life. We thought it was worth setting out the current position clearly.

How are you seeing this play out in your referrals and prescribing? We are interested in how widely the first-line SLT recommendation has filtered through to primary care.
linkedInHook: For decades, eye drops were the default first step in glaucoma. The evidence has moved. Here is where first-line treatment stands now.
notes:
SLIDES (10):

Slide 1: The case for SLT as first-line treatment in newly diagnosed glaucoma. Where the evidence stands now.

Slide 2: The traditional pathway. For many years, newly diagnosed open-angle glaucoma was managed first with daily pressure-lowering eye drops, with laser or surgery reserved for later.

Slide 3: What changed. The LiGHT trial (Gazzard et al, Lancet 2019) compared selective laser trabeculoplasty (SLT) with eye drops as first-line treatment in open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension.

Slide 4: What the LiGHT trial found. SLT as first-line treatment was at least as effective as drops at controlling pressure, with more patients achieving target pressure without the need for daily medication, and with cost and quality of life advantages.

Slide 5: What the guidance says. NICE guideline NG81 now recommends offering SLT as a first-line treatment option for people with newly diagnosed open-angle glaucoma and ocular hypertension.

Slide 6: Why it matters for patients. Daily drops depend on compliance, which is difficult to sustain over years. They can cause local side effects. SLT is a single outpatient procedure that lowers pressure without the daily burden, and it can be repeated if needed.

Slide 7: The honest caveats. SLT is not suitable for every patient or every type of glaucoma. It does not always remove the need for drops entirely. The right first step is still an individual clinical decision.

Slide 8: What this means for referrals. A newly diagnosed open-angle glaucoma patient can now reasonably be offered SLT as a first option. It is worth knowing this when discussing the pathway with your patients.

Slide 9: SLT at CES Medical. We offer SLT as a five-minute outpatient procedure at Chatham and Headcorn. We are happy to discuss whether a specific patient is a suitable candidate.

Slide 10: CES Medical. Glaucoma services across Kent. cesmedical.co.uk

title: Five common misconceptions about eyelid surgery
caption:
Eyelid surgery is one of the most misunderstood procedures we discuss with patients and referrers. The biggest misconception is that it is always cosmetic, when for many patients a drooping upper eyelid is a medical condition affecting their vision.

Here are five things worth clarifying, for patients considering it and for colleagues who may be the first to see these patients.

If you have a patient whose drooping eyelid is affecting their visual field, it may be a funded medical procedure rather than a cosmetic one. We are happy to advise.

Which of these comes up most in your conversations with patients?
linkedInHook: The most common misconception about eyelid surgery is that it is always cosmetic. For many patients, it is a medical procedure that restores their field of vision.
notes:
SLIDES (10):

Slide 1: Five common misconceptions about eyelid surgery.

Slide 2: Misconception one: it is always cosmetic. In fact, when a drooping upper eyelid (ptosis) cuts into the upper field of vision, it is a functional medical condition. Functional eyelid surgery is a different pathway to cosmetic blepharoplasty.

Slide 3: Misconception two: insurance never covers it. When the eyelid is affecting vision, many private medical insurers do cover functional eyelid surgery. The functional versus cosmetic distinction is what determines this.

Slide 4: Misconception three: it is a major operation. Eyelid surgery is usually performed under local anaesthetic as a day case. Most patients describe pressure rather than pain.

Slide 5: Misconception four: the scars will be obvious. Incisions are placed in the natural crease of the eyelid. Scarring is usually minimal and fades over the months following surgery.

Slide 6: Misconception five: recovery takes months. Bruising and swelling settle over one to two weeks. Most people return to normal activities within a couple of weeks, though recovery varies and your surgeon will advise you individually.

Slide 7: The one that matters most. If a drooping eyelid is affecting vision, it may be a medical condition, not a cosmetic concern. That distinction changes the pathway and often the funding.

Slide 8: Our oculoplastic care. Mr Nick Kopsachilis, Consultant Ophthalmic Surgeon, leads our oculoplastic service. We assess whether an eyelid concern is functional or cosmetic at consultation, with no assumption and no pressure.

Slide 9: CES Medical provides oculoplastic surgery at Headcorn and Chatham. To discuss a patient or book an assessment, call 01732 757771.

title: Diabetic retinopathy: what the screening result means in practice
caption:
Every person with diabetes in England is offered annual diabetic eye screening. The result comes back as a grade, and patients often do not understand what it means, while GPs may not always have time to explain it.

This is a short reference on what the diabetic eye screening grades mean in practice, for primary care colleagues and for patients trying to make sense of their result.

CES Medical works closely with the diabetic eye screening pathway. Mr Syed Shahid, our Consultant Ophthalmologist and Vitreoretinal Surgeon, is the Clinical Lead for the Kent and Medway Diabetic Eye Screening Programme.

How well do you feel the screening grades are understood by your patients? We are interested in where the communication gaps are.
linkedInHook: Your diabetic patient comes back from their eye screening with a grade on the letter. Here is what each grade actually means for them, and when it matters.
notes:
SLIDES (10):

Slide 1: Diabetic retinopathy: what the screening result means in practice.

Slide 2: The basics. Everyone with diabetes aged 12 and over is offered annual diabetic eye screening. The retina is photographed and graded for signs of diabetic retinopathy and maculopathy.

Slide 3: R0: no retinopathy. No diabetic changes detected. Continue annual screening. Reassuring.

Slide 4: R1: background retinopathy. Early, mild changes. Very common in people who have had diabetes for some years. Not an emergency. Continues in routine annual screening with good diabetes control.

Slide 5: R2: pre-proliferative retinopathy. More significant changes that need closer monitoring. Referred for specialist assessment, usually routine but timely.

Slide 6: R3: proliferative retinopathy. New abnormal blood vessels have developed. This needs prompt specialist assessment and often treatment to protect sight.

Slide 7: M1: maculopathy. Changes near the macula, the central vision area. Referred for specialist assessment because the central vision is at risk.

Slide 8: The practical takeaway. R0 and R1 are managed within routine screening with good diabetes control. R2, R3 and M1 mean specialist referral. Good glucose and blood pressure control slows progression at every stage.

Slide 9: How CES fits in. We provide specialist assessment and treatment for referable diabetic eye disease. Mr Syed Shahid leads the Kent and Medway Diabetic Eye Screening Programme and our vitreoretinal service. We work with the screening pathway, not around it.

Slide 10: CES Medical. Vitreoretinal and diabetic eye services across Kent. cesmedical.co.uk

title: Dry eye: when IPL is worth considering
caption:
Dry eye is one of the most common presentations in optometry and primary care, and most of it is managed well with conservative measures. This post is about the subset of patients for whom drops and lid hygiene are not enough, and where IPL may be worth considering.

It is written for optometry and primary care colleagues who see these patients regularly and want a clear sense of when a referral for IPL is appropriate.

We are always happy to discuss a specific patient before a formal referral.

Where do you currently draw the line between continued conservative management and onward referral for dry eye?
linkedInHook: Most dry eye is managed well with drops, lid hygiene and warm compresses. This is about the patients for whom that is not enough.
notes:
SLIDES (10):

Slide 1: Dry eye: when IPL is worth considering. A referral guide for primary care and optometry.

Slide 2: First, the obvious. Most dry eye is managed well in the community: artificial tears, lid hygiene, warm compresses, addressing screen habits and environment. This remains the right first step.

Slide 3: The subset that struggles. Some patients, particularly those with meibomian gland dysfunction (MGD), do not get sustained relief from conservative measures. Their dry eye is evaporative, driven by poor quality of the tear film oil layer.

Slide 4: What IPL does. Intense Pulsed Light targets the meibomian glands, helping to restore the oil layer of the tear film. It addresses the underlying MGD rather than supplementing tears. Supported by clinical evidence.

Slide 5: When to consider referral for IPL. Patients with confirmed MGD. Patients who have not achieved sustained relief from conservative management over a reasonable period. Patients whose dry eye materially affects daily function such as screen work, reading or driving.

Slide 6: When IPL is not the answer. Aqueous-deficient dry eye without significant MGD. Certain skin types and conditions that require assessment first. IPL is not suitable for every patient, and suitability is confirmed at assessment.

Slide 7: What it involves for the patient. A series of sessions, typically three to four, spaced a few weeks apart. Non-invasive. No downtime.

Slide 8: How to refer or discuss. We are happy to discuss a specific patient informally before a formal referral. If you have a patient who has exhausted conservative measures, a conversation may be quicker than you expect.

Slide 9: IPL at CES Medical. We assess suitability and deliver IPL as a private treatment for MGD-related dry eye. We work alongside your existing management, not instead of it.

Slide 10: CES Medical. Dry eye and ocular surface services across Kent. cesmedical.co.uk

title: What FRCOphth means, and what to look for in an eye surgeon's credentials
caption:
Patients often see letters after a surgeon's name without knowing what they signify. For GPs and optometrists advising patients, and for patients themselves, it helps to understand what these credentials actually represent.

This is a short guide to FRCOphth and the equivalent senior qualifications held by internationally trained consultants, and how anyone can verify a surgeon's standing.

How often do your patients ask you about a consultant's qualifications when choosing where to be referred?
linkedInHook: When a patient asks how they know their eye surgeon is properly qualified, the answer involves more than one set of letters. Here is what they mean.
notes:
SLIDES (10):

Slide 1: What FRCOphth means, and what to look for in an eye surgeon's credentials.

Slide 2: FRCOphth stands for Fellow of the Royal College of Ophthalmologists. It is the highest professional qualification for eye surgeons trained through the UK pathway.

Slide 3: What it requires. Years of supervised surgical training, written and practical examinations, and ongoing revalidation. It is not honorary. It is earned and maintained.

Slide 4: But FRCOphth is not the only route. Many excellent consultants trained outside the UK and hold equivalent senior qualifications, such as an MD, a PhD, or European board certification (FEBO). These represent rigorous training through a different system.

Slide 5: What matters for the patient. Not a single set of letters, but that the surgeon has completed the full specialist training their field demands and is registered to practise. UK and internationally trained consultants both meet that bar.

Slide 6: How to verify. Every doctor practising in the UK is on the General Medical Council (GMC) register, which anyone can search. It confirms registration, specialty and any conditions on practice.

Slide 7: At CES Medical. Our consultant surgeons include Fellows of the Royal College of Ophthalmologists and internationally trained consultants with senior qualifications such as MD and PhD. Every one has completed the rigorous training their specialty demands.

Slide 8: The takeaway. Credentials are worth understanding, but the question is not which letters a surgeon has. It is whether they have completed full specialist training and are properly registered. Ours have, and are.

Slide 9: You can see each of our consultants' qualifications on their profile pages. cesmedical.co.uk/consultants

title: Why I started CES Medical
caption:
I started CES Medical in 2018 because I kept watching the same thing happen.

A patient would come in for a routine appointment. I would identify something that needed specialist attention. I would refer them. And then I would hear back, months later, that they were still waiting.

Not because the system did not care. Because the specialist capacity in Kent was not where the patients were.

I had trained in optometry, in clinical neuroscience, in glaucoma management. I understood what good looked like. And I knew that the patients in Chatham and Headcorn and Tunbridge Wells deserved the same access to it as someone who could get themselves to London.

So we built it here instead.

Six years later, I still think about that gap. Every new clinic, every new consultant, every new service is an answer to the same question: why should where you live determine the quality of your eye care.

It should not. That is what we are trying to fix.

What made you choose the work you are in? I am genuinely curious whether this kind of frustration is what drives most people in healthcare.
linkedInHook: I started CES Medical in 2018 because I kept watching the same thing happen.

title: What consultant-led actually means
caption:
Consultant-led is a phrase that gets used a lot in private healthcare. I want to be specific about what it means at CES Medical because it is not a marketing phrase for us.

It means the consultant who sees you at your first appointment is the consultant who performs your surgery.

That matters for several reasons. The surgeon knows exactly what they found at assessment. They made the decision to operate based on what they saw personally. They know your specific anatomy before they enter the theatre. There is no handover, no interpretation of another clinician's notes, no adjustment on the day.

In a non-consultant-led model, assessment and surgery can be performed by different clinicians. That is common. It is not necessarily wrong. But for procedures on something as precise as the human eye, continuity of care has real clinical value.

This is why we built CES Medical around it. Not because it sounded better. Because it produces better outcomes.

Does this distinction matter to you in how you refer patients or choose providers? I would be interested in whether GPs and optometrists weigh this when making decisions.
linkedInHook: Consultant-led is a phrase that gets used a lot in private healthcare.
notes: PLACEHOLDER. Elion to rewrite in his own voice. Post at 8am Tuesday.

title: The real reason patients delay eye treatment
caption:
People assume patients delay eye treatment because of cost or fear of surgery.

In my experience, the most common reason is much simpler. They do not know if they are bad enough yet.

They have adapted to their vision changing gradually. They have stopped driving at night and told themselves it is a preference, not a problem. They have moved to larger print and told themselves they just need stronger glasses. They have quietly accepted a narrowing world and called it ageing.

They are not avoiding us. They do not know they need us.

This changes how we communicate. We do not spend much time trying to convince people who are ready to act. We spend most of our time helping people recognise that the question they have been sitting on for two years is worth asking out loud.

That is why we publish what we publish. Not to sell. To give someone permission to pick up the phone.

Does this match what you see in your practice? How do you handle the patient who has been managing fine for much longer than they should have been?
linkedInHook: People assume patients delay eye treatment because of cost or fear of surgery.
notes: PLACEHOLDER. Elion to rewrite in his own voice. Post at 8am Tuesday.

title: Why CES treats both NHS and private patients
caption:
Some people ask why CES Medical works across both NHS and private pathways.

We are an NHS-commissioned provider in Kent. Eye disease does not discriminate by income. Cataracts, glaucoma and diabetic retinopathy affect people across every demographic in this county. We believe specialist ophthalmology should be available to all of them.

Our NHS patients are seen by the same consultants, in the same facilities, to the same clinical standards as our private patients. For cataract patients, the difference is access speed, premium lens options and consultation time. For other conditions, private care opens access to services and treatments that fall outside NHS commissioning entirely. Not the quality of the surgeon or the surgery.

We made this a founding principle of CES Medical and we have not moved from it. Commercial pressure will always push a healthcare provider with a private offer towards its most profitable patient group. We resist that because we think it is the wrong way to build healthcare.

I am genuinely interested in whether others in healthcare see NHS and private as complementary or competing. And whether commercial pressures have changed that view over the past five years.

title: How I chose the consultants at CES Medical
caption:
People assume clinical decisions are made on reputation and referral networks.

For me it started with a different question: what kind of doctor talks to their patient during surgery.

I have seen enough operative environments to know that technical skill is table stakes at the level we were recruiting. What differentiates surgeons at the top of their field is how they treat the person on the table. Whether they explain what is happening. Whether they notice anxiety and respond to it. Whether the patient feels held as well as treated.

Every consultant at CES Medical was chosen partly on that basis. Not because it is soft or commercial. Because a patient having surgery on their eye is in one of the most vulnerable positions they will ever be in. The person doing that surgery should know it.

How do you evaluate clinical culture when you are building a team? I am genuinely curious whether others use similar criteria or whether this is an unusual way to make those decisions.

title: What expanding CES to Southborough actually means
caption:
For some time we have been able to see patients in the Tunbridge Wells area for consultations at our Pantiles clinic. But when those patients needed surgery, they had to travel to Chatham or Headcorn for the procedure.

For most people that is manageable. For an older patient, someone who does not drive, or someone relying on family to take a day off work to get them there and back, it is a real barrier. And it is exactly the kind of barrier that quietly stops people from getting treatment they need.

Opening our Southborough site changes that. It means patients in the Tunbridge Wells area can now have their surgery locally, close to home, rather than travelling across the county for it.

This is the part of expansion I care about. Not the number of sites. Not growth for its own sake. The question I keep coming back to is simple: is there a group of patients in Kent who are going without, or making do, because of where they happen to live? Southborough answers that for one part of the county.

As an NHS-commissioned provider, we think about geographic access the way the NHS has to. Where are the gaps. Who is underserved. What would make the difference for them. Private capacity and NHS commissioning can work together to close those gaps rather than compete.

There is more of Kent to think about after this. But the principle stays the same. Expansion should follow patient need, not the other way around.

I am interested in how others in healthcare think about geographic expansion. Do you start with the patient gap, or with the commercial opportunity? And can the two ever genuinely point in the same direction?

title: What a clinical optometrist sees that a standard eye test misses
caption:
A GP once told me he did not know there was a difference between a clinical optometrist and a high street optician until a patient of his came back from a CES assessment with a glaucoma diagnosis that had been missed at three previous routine tests.

The difference is training and scope. A community optometrist checks your prescription, screens for obvious pathology and refers when something is outside their competence. A specialist clinical optometrist with independent prescribing qualifications can diagnose, initiate treatment, monitor complex conditions and manage the patient longitudinally without a GP or ophthalmologist in the loop for every decision.

I hold a professional certificate in glaucoma and an independent prescribing qualification. That means I can assess a patient presenting with raised IOP, make a clinical decision about whether they need treatment, initiate drops if appropriate and monitor them over time. That is a fundamentally different scope to a standard optometry appointment.

This matters in a system under pressure. Not every patient with a glaucoma suspect result needs to sit on an ophthalmology waiting list. Some of them can be safely managed in a community optometry setting with the right qualifications. Others need onward referral. The skill is knowing which is which.

Does this distinction come up in your practice? I am curious how many GPs are aware of the difference in scope between optometrists at different levels.

title: The hardest part of building CES Medical
caption:
People assume the hard part of building a healthcare business is the funding, or the recruitment, or the regulation. Those were hard. But they were the kind of hard I expected.

The part I underestimated was changing what people believed was possible near where they live.

For years, patients in this part of Kent assumed that anything beyond a routine eye test meant a long wait or a trip to London. That belief was so settled that even when we could offer consultant-led care locally, people did not quite believe it applied to them. They would ask whether they needed to go to London for the surgery we were offering two miles from their home.

You cannot fix that with marketing. You fix it slowly, one patient at a time, by doing what you said you would do and letting them tell the next person. It is the slowest part of the whole thing and there is no shortcut I have found.

I still do not think we have fully changed it. There are people in Kent today who are travelling further or waiting longer than they need to, because they do not know there is another option locally. That sits with me more than any of the operational challenges did.

For others who have built something in healthcare or in their community: what did you find unexpectedly hard? Not the thing you tell people about. The thing that actually kept you up.

title: What treating thousands of patients a year teaches you
caption:
When you see a small number of patients, every case feels unique. When you see many thousands a year, something changes. You start to recognise patterns you would never have seen otherwise.

[FIGURE TO CONFIRM: across CES Medical we now see in the region of 13,000 patient episodes a year. Confirm this number is accurate before posting. If it is not, adjust or remove it.]

That volume does something to how you practise. The unusual presentation that a clinician might see once in a decade, we might see several times a year. The subtle sign that is easy to dismiss in isolation becomes familiar. You learn what is genuinely rare and what only feels rare because you have not seen enough of it.

I am cautious about how I say this, because it is not that any individual clinician is better or worse. It is that scale builds a particular kind of pattern recognition, and that pattern recognition changes the questions you ask and the things you notice.

It also changes how you design a service. When you see enough patients, the gaps in the pathway stop being anecdotes and start being data. You can see where people fall through, where they wait too long, where the system could be smoother.

For others working at scale in healthcare: what has volume taught you that you could not have learned any other way?

title: Why we invested in precision measurement
caption:
When people think about cataract surgery, they think about the operation itself. The part that determines the result often happens before the patient is anywhere near the theatre.

Before any lens is chosen, the eye has to be measured. Its length, the curvature of the cornea, the depth of the chamber. Those measurements determine which lens, and which lens power, will give the patient the vision they are hoping for. Get the measurement slightly wrong and the lens choice follows it, and the patient notices.

That is why we invested in the Zeiss IOLMaster 700 for our biometry. It is not the visible part of the service. No patient ever chose us because of a measurement device. But it is one of the quiet decisions that shapes whether someone gets the outcome they were promised.

I think about investment this way. The things worth spending on are often the things the patient never sees, because those are the things that determine whether the visible parts work.

This matters more, not less, for premium lenses. A multifocal or toric lens is less forgiving of a measurement error than a standard lens. The precision has to be there for the technology to deliver what it promises.

For others making decisions about where to invest in a clinical service: how do you weigh the things patients can see against the things they cannot?

title: Why corneal transplantation is one of the most technically demanding procedures in ophthalmology
caption:
Corneal transplantation has changed dramatically in the past decade. We have moved away from full-thickness grafts towards selective lamellar procedures that transplant only the diseased layer of the cornea rather than the entire structure.

DMEK (Descemet Membrane Endothelial Keratoplasty) is the most technically refined of these. The tissue being transplanted is a single cell layer, approximately 10 to 15 microns thick. To put that in context, a human hair is around 70 microns wide. The graft has no structural rigidity. It must be inserted into the eye through a small incision, unfolded without damage and positioned precisely against the recipient cornea using an air bubble.

The learning curve is steep. Outcomes in experienced hands are significantly better than in lower-volume centres. For patients with Fuchs' endothelial dystrophy or bullous keratopathy, this distinction matters.

I completed my doctoral research on corneal endothelial cell biology before specialising in transplantation. That foundation changes how I think about every operative decision, not just the technical steps but the cellular logic behind them.

If you are an optometrist or GP seeing patients with progressive corneal oedema or unexplained visual decline that does not respond to refraction, I am happy to discuss cases informally before a formal referral is made.

What is your experience of the referral pathway for corneal conditions? I am interested in whether the current system is identifying these patients early enough.
linkedInHook: Corneal transplantation has changed dramatically in the past decade.

title: Multifocal lens implants: what patients are not always told before they decide
caption:
Multifocal intraocular lenses are one of the most significant advances in cataract surgery of the past twenty years. For appropriately selected patients, the chance of being completely glasses-free after surgery is real and the outcomes are excellent.

But I want to be direct about something that does not always get enough weight in patient-facing communications.

Multifocal lenses divide light. They create multiple focal points simultaneously. For most patients, the visual cortex adapts and the brain learns to select the relevant focus for the task. For some patients, it does not. Those patients experience halos, glare and reduced contrast sensitivity, particularly in low light. Night driving can become genuinely difficult.

The patient who asks me will I need glasses after my cataract surgery is asking the right question. But the question I need to answer first is are you the right patient for a lens that might eliminate glasses but might also change how you see at night.

That requires an honest conversation about lifestyle, expectations, driving habits, occupational visual demands and the clinical characteristics of their eye. It is not a five-minute decision.

At CES we take that conversation seriously because recommending a multifocal lens to the wrong patient creates a problem that is very difficult to reverse.

What do you observe in patients who have had multifocal lenses fitted elsewhere and are presenting with visual complaints? I am interested in whether this is a pattern others are seeing.
linkedInHook: Multifocal intraocular lenses are one of the most significant advances in cataract surgery of the past twenty years.
notes: Mr Kopsachilis to review and post from his personal account.

title: What dry AMD patients are not being told
caption:
When a patient receives a dry AMD diagnosis, the standard conversation has historically covered three things: lifestyle modification, AREDS supplementation and monitoring.

That conversation is not wrong. But I think it has become a ceiling that stops both clinicians and patients from asking the next question.

Dry AMD is not a static condition. It progresses. And for patients who are watching their central vision slowly narrow over years, being told there is nothing to treat is a significant burden to carry.

I have spent a considerable part of my career in medical retina, and I am cautious about overclaiming for emerging treatments. The evidence base for some newer approaches is still developing. But I also think patients have a right to know that the picture is more complex than monitor and wait.

If you have patients with early to intermediate dry AMD who have been told there are no options, a conversation with a medical retina specialist who is familiar with the full range of current approaches is worth having. Not because every patient will be a candidate for something different. But because some will be, and they deserve to know.

I am not making specific treatment claims in a LinkedIn post. What I am saying is that the referral threshold for a specialist opinion on dry AMD should be lower than it currently is in many settings.

I see patients with dry AMD at Headcorn, Southborough, Chatham and Pantiles as part of CES Medical.

Has this come up in your practice? I would be interested in whether GPs and optometrists feel they have enough information to have this conversation with their patients.
linkedInHook: When a patient receives a dry AMD diagnosis, the standard conversation has historically covered three things.

title: What the revalidation process for ophthalmologists actually involves
caption:
I served as Co-Lead for Revalidation at the Royal College of Ophthalmologists for four years. I want to explain what revalidation actually involves because I think patients and referring clinicians often have a vague sense of it as a box-ticking exercise. It is not.

Every consultant in the UK undergoes annual appraisal and five-yearly revalidation. To revalidate, a consultant must demonstrate: continuing professional development across the breadth of their practice, clinical outcome data, multi-source feedback from colleagues and patients, a record of significant events and complaints with evidence of reflection and learning and confirmation that they have no health or conduct issues affecting their practice.

The appraisal process requires a consultant to sit with an appraiser and work through all of this documentation in detail. It is not a rubber stamp.

When I held this role at the Royal College, I was responsible, alongside my co-lead, for ensuring that the standards applied to ophthalmologists across the country were meaningful. That experience changed how I think about my own practice. You cannot set rigorous standards for others and then hold yourself to a lower bar.

When a patient asks me how they know their surgeon is safe to practise, revalidation is part of the answer. Not the whole answer. But a real and important part.

Does the revalidation process come up in conversations with patients when they are choosing a provider? I am curious whether this is something patients are beginning to ask about directly.
linkedInHook: I served as Co-Lead for Revalidation at the Royal College of Ophthalmologists for four years.

title: Retinal detachment: the window that most patients do not know exists
caption:
The visual outcome from retinal detachment surgery is substantially better when the macula is still attached at the time of the operation.

Once the detachment reaches the macula, the central vision is affected. Surgery can still repair the retina, but the recovery of central vision is less predictable and often incomplete.

The challenge is that patients experiencing the warning signs of retinal detachment frequently do not understand the urgency. A sudden shower of floaters and flashes is frightening, but it does not feel like an emergency in the way that chest pain does. People wait to see if it settles. They book an appointment with their optician. They look it up online.

The signs that need same-day assessment: a sudden significant increase in floaters, flashes of light particularly in peripheral vision, or a shadow or curtain moving across the visual field. Any of these, particularly in combination, need to be assessed urgently. Not in a few days. That day.

For GPs: a patient presenting with these symptoms should be directed to an eye casualty department immediately, not referred in the routine pathway.

I am one of a small number of vitreoretinal surgeons in Kent. I see the outcomes difference that speed makes. It is real and it is significant.

What is your experience of patients presenting late with retinal symptoms? I would like to understand where the delay most commonly occurs.
linkedInHook: The visual outcome from retinal detachment surgery is substantially better when the macula is still attached at the time of the operation.
notes: Mr Shahid to review and post from his personal account.

title: What a vitreoretinal surgeon actually does and why having one in Kent matters
caption:
Vitreoretinal surgery covers the back of the eye: the retina, the vitreous gel and the structures that support them.

The conditions that fall within this subspecialty include retinal detachment, macular hole, epiretinal membrane, vitreous haemorrhage, diabetic vitreoretinal disease and some forms of trauma. These are not common conditions in absolute terms. But when they occur, they are often sight-threatening and time-sensitive.

Vitreoretinal surgery requires subspecialty training beyond general ophthalmology. I completed an 18-month fellowship at Moorfields Eye Hospital specifically in this area, where the volume and complexity of cases provides an exposure that is difficult to replicate elsewhere.

The reason this matters geographically is that vitreoretinal surgeons are not evenly distributed. In many parts of the country, patients needing this surgery travel significant distances or wait for capacity at major centres. For time-sensitive conditions like retinal detachment, that waiting is not always clinically safe.

Being based in Kent with this subspecialty means that patients in Southborough, Chatham and across the region have access to this level of care closer to home.

If you have patients you are uncertain about referring, or conditions that you think might benefit from a vitreoretinal opinion, I am happy to discuss informally before a formal referral is made.

What is your current pathway for suspected retinal conditions? I would like to understand what the referral experience looks like from a GP or optometrist perspective in Kent.
linkedInHook: Vitreoretinal surgery covers the back of the eye: the retina, the vitreous gel and the structures that support them.

title: Keratoconus: the early signs, and why they are easy to miss
caption:
Keratoconus is one of those conditions where the earlier we catch it, the more we can do, and where the early signs are genuinely easy to miss.

It usually appears in the teens or twenties. The cornea gradually thins and steepens, distorting vision. In the early stages it can look like an ordinary refractive change, which is exactly what makes it easy to overlook.

The signs worth a second thought: a prescription that keeps changing, particularly increasing or irregular astigmatism. Best-corrected acuity that is not quite as good as it should be. Subtle asymmetry between the two eyes. None of these is dramatic on its own, and that is the point.

Optometrists are the first line here. You see these patients regularly and you see their refractive changes over time, which is precisely the vantage point from which early keratoconus reveals itself. When something does not quite add up, corneal topography is the next step.

Why it matters so much: in early keratoconus, corneal cross-linking can halt progression. Caught early, we can often preserve the cornea the patient has. Caught late, the options narrow, and for some patients that eventually means a corneal transplant. The earlier the referral, the more of the patient's own cornea we can protect.

If you have a patient whose refractive picture is not behaving as expected, I am happy to discuss before a formal referral.

What makes you reach for topography in a younger patient with shifting astigmatism? I am interested in how early the suspicion tends to form in practice.
linkedInHook: Keratoconus is one of those conditions where the earlier we catch it, the more we can do, and where the early signs are genuinely easy to miss.

title: The moment you realise your vision has changed
caption:
There is usually a specific moment when it becomes undeniable.

For some people it is a car journey at night when the oncoming headlights suddenly seem unbearable. For others it is trying to read a menu in a restaurant and holding it further and further away. For others it is a grandchild pointing at something in the distance that they simply cannot see.

These moments are not signs of getting old. They are often signs of a cataract developing.

Cataracts are gradual. They are also very treatable. If you have had one of those moments recently, it is worth a conversation.

Call us on 01732 757771. No obligation, no rush.

Have you had a moment like this? We would love to hear from you in the comments.

title: What surgery day actually looks like
caption:
We hear the same fears every week.

"Will I be awake?" Yes, but patients do not feel pain. Local anaesthetic drops are used throughout.
"Will I see what is happening?" No. Patients see light and movement but not the procedure itself.
"What if I move my eye?" The team is trained for this. It is more common than you think and it does not affect the outcome.
"How long does it take?" Around 15 to 20 minutes per eye.
"Can my partner come?" Yes. They can wait in our reception and be with you immediately after.

Surgery on your eye is frightening to think about. Most patients tell us it was much more manageable than they expected.

If you have a question that is keeping you from making the first call, ask us.

Call 01732 757771 or comment below and we will come back to you.

title: Five signs your parent might have a cataract they have not mentioned
caption:
If your mum or dad is over 65, there is a reasonable chance they have a cataract developing that they have not told you about. Not because they are hiding it. Because it happened so gradually they did not notice.

Here are five things to look for.

Swipe through and save this one.
notes:
Carousel. 7 slides. Warm family-oriented cover image — adult child with older parent, relaxed setting, not clinical. Written to be shared by adult children with their parents. This is the highest share-potential post in the campaign. The fifth sign (reading habits) was added to fulfil the "five signs" promise in the title. Do not reduce back to four slides. "Save this one" in the caption is a save prompt, not engagement bait.

Slide 1: Five signs your parent might have a cataract they have not mentioned.
Slide 2: They have stopped driving at night. They probably said it is a preference. It may not be.
Slide 3: The television is louder than it used to be. When vision becomes difficult, people often compensate in other ways without realising it.
Slide 4: They are holding things further away to read. This is often mistaken for a reading glasses issue. It can be both.
Slide 5: They have stopped reading for pleasure or switched to large-print books. Patients often attribute this to preference rather than difficulty. It is worth asking about.
Slide 6: They seem less confident in unfamiliar places. Reduced contrast sensitivity from a cataract affects how clearly the world appears, making navigation harder. This is often attributed to age.
Slide 7: A simple eye test at their optician will show whether a cataract is developing. If it is, call us on 01732 757771 and our patient coordinator will help you find the right next step for them.

title: Drooping eyelids and vision
caption:
A drooping upper eyelid is not always a cosmetic concern.

When the lid drops far enough to cut into your field of vision, it becomes a medical condition. Patients describe it as: feeling like they are always looking from under a heavy brow, difficulty reading for long periods, needing to lift their chin to see clearly.

If this sounds familiar, it is worth a specialist assessment. In many cases it is covered by private medical insurance.

Mr Nick Kopsachilis, our oculoplastic specialist, sees patients at Tunbridge Wells and Headcorn. Call 01732 757771 to find out whether your eyelid condition is functional or cosmetic.

title: What happens after cataract surgery
caption:
One of the most common questions we get is "what happens after surgery?"

Here is an honest guide to the first two weeks. Save this for before your appointment.
notes:

Slide 1: What to expect after cataract surgery. The honest guide.

Slide 2: Day 1. Your eye may feel gritty or scratchy. Vision may be blurry. This is normal. Rest at home. Use your drops as instructed.

Slide 3: Days 2 to 4. Vision usually begins to settle. Avoid rubbing the eye. Most people manage gentle daily activities.

Slide 4: Day 7. Your follow-up appointment. Your surgeon checks the eye and assesses your vision. Your surgeon will advise when it is safe for you to drive again.

Slide 5: Week 2 onwards. Avoid swimming and heavy lifting for a few weeks. Most people are back to their usual routine within a couple of weeks, though your surgeon will confirm what is right for you.

Slide 6: Not sure if something is normal? Our 24-hour post-operative helpline is 01732 756789. Available from the day of your surgery.

title: Glaucoma and family history
caption:
If one of your parents had glaucoma, your risk of developing it is three to four times higher than average.

Glaucoma has no symptoms in its early stages. It damages your peripheral vision slowly, over years, without pain. By the time most people notice something is wrong, some damage has already occurred.

The good news is that caught early, glaucoma is very manageable. Drops, laser treatment and surgery are all available depending on the stage and type.

If you have a family history of glaucoma and have not had a full eye examination in the past two years, that is where to start.

Your optician can screen for early signs. If they refer you to us, we will see you promptly.

Call 01732 757771.

title: Your questions about eyelid surgery, answered honestly
caption:
We get asked about eyelid surgery more than almost anything else on our social media.

Here are the five most common questions, answered honestly.
notes:
Carousel. 6 slides. Facebook. High save potential. FAQ format performs well with the Facebook older adult audience who prefer clear, structured information.

This is the Facebook companion to P91 (LinkedIn eyelid misconceptions carousel). Both lead with the functional versus cosmetic distinction. P80 is the patient-facing FAQ version; P91 is the more clinical LinkedIn version for patients and referrers.

CLINICAL ACCURACY: Recovery information is honest with no fixed guarantee, consistent with P76 and P154. Oculoplastic surgery is performed at Headcorn and Chatham, not Pantiles (consultation only).

Slide 1: Your questions about eyelid surgery. Answered honestly.

Slide 2: Is it painful? Eyelid surgery is performed under local anaesthetic. Patients typically describe it as pressure rather than pain.

Slide 3: Will I have visible scars? Incisions are made in the natural crease of the eyelid. Scarring is usually minimal and fades significantly over the months following surgery.

Slide 4: Is it cosmetic or medical? This is the most important question. If a drooping eyelid is affecting your field of vision, it is a medical condition, not a cosmetic one. That distinction matters, and it is often why the procedure is covered by private medical insurance. We can help you check your policy before you book.

Slide 5: How long is the recovery? Bruising and swelling usually settle over one to two weeks. Stitches, if needed, typically come out around seven days. Your surgeon will give you advice specific to you.

Slide 6: How do I know if I need it? That is what the consultation is for. Our oculoplastic specialist Mr Nick Kopsachilis sees patients across Kent. Call 01732 757771 to book an assessment. No obligation to proceed.

Tone: warm, clear, honest. The 65-plus Facebook audience prefers structured, reassuring information.

title: Three months of CES Medical on Facebook
caption:
Three months ago we started sharing more openly on here. The questions you sent us, the comments you left, the messages you sent asking things you had been wondering about for years.

Thank you.

title: Lens options for cataract surgery: the honest comparison
caption:
Monofocal. Multifocal. EDOF. Toric.

If you are researching cataract surgery, the lens conversation can feel overwhelming. Here is an honest breakdown. Save this before your consultation.
notes:
Carousel. 7 slides. Instagram. High save potential. Designed to be returned to before a consultation.

Slide 1: Which cataract lens is right for you? The honest breakdown.

Slide 2: Monofocal lens. Corrects one distance, usually distance vision. Most patients still need reading glasses for close-up tasks. Some patients choose near focus depending on their occupation. The most established option. Included in standard cataract surgery.

Slide 3: Multifocal lens. Corrects multiple distances. Many patients find they need glasses far less or not at all. Not for everyone: some experience haloes or glare at night. Requires clinical assessment to confirm suitability.

Slide 4: EDOF (Extended Depth of Focus). Extended intermediate range. Fewer haloes than full multifocal. May still need reading glasses for fine print. A middle-ground option for many patients.

Slide 5: Toric lens. Corrects distance vision and astigmatism at the same time. Astigmatism is a common condition where the cornea is slightly irregular, causing blurry or distorted vision. A toric design can also be combined with multifocal technology for patients who want to correct both astigmatism and reading distance. Your consultant will advise at assessment.

Slide 6: Multifocal vs varifocal glasses. Not the same thing. A multifocal IOL is a surgical implant inside the eye. A varifocal is spectacle lenses. Confusing the two is common. A varifocal prescription will not replace a multifocal IOL, and vice versa. Your consultant will explain what combination suits you.

Slide 7: The right lens is the one that fits your life, not the one that looks best on a chart. That conversation happens at your consultation. cespatientinformation.co.uk

title: Glaucoma: everything you need to know if you have just been diagnosed
caption:
If you or someone you love has just been told they have glaucoma, this is for you.

It is a condition that sounds frightening and is frequently misunderstood. Swipe through for what it actually means, what the options are and what life with managed glaucoma looks like.

Save this.
notes:
Carousel. 9 slides. Instagram. High save and share potential. Designed to be shared by newly diagnosed patients with family members. Warm but authoritative. No alarm, no overclaiming on outcomes.

PDF or image carousel. If posted to LinkedIn as well, no video, so LinkedIn-compatible as a document carousel.

Slide 1: Just diagnosed with glaucoma? Here is what it actually means.

Slide 2: Glaucoma is not one condition. It is a group of conditions that damage the optic nerve, usually through raised pressure inside the eye.

Slide 3: The most important thing to know: caught early, glaucoma is very manageable. The damage cannot be reversed but it can be slowed significantly.

Slide 4: Why you may not have noticed symptoms. Glaucoma typically affects peripheral vision first. The brain compensates so well that most people do not notice until a screening test picks it up.

Slide 5: What management looks like. Eye drops (most common first step). SLT laser (five-minute outpatient procedure, very effective at lowering pressure). Surgery for advanced cases.

Slide 6: About SLT laser at CES Medical. Selective Laser Trabeculoplasty is a quick, well-tolerated procedure that lowers eye pressure without daily drop compliance. It is increasingly offered as a first-line option.

Slide 7: What monitoring involves. Regular appointments to check eye pressure, optic nerve imaging and visual field testing. Usually every six to twelve months depending on stability.

Slide 8: Does it mean I will go blind? For most patients managed appropriately from diagnosis: no. Sight loss from glaucoma is largely preventable with treatment.

Slide 9: We see glaucoma patients at Chatham, Headcorn and Southborough. cespatientinformation.co.uk or call 01732 757771.

Tone: warm, authoritative, reassuring. This post is designed to be shared by a newly diagnosed patient with their worried family. Slide 8 directly answers the question they are all thinking.
